column1: People | Context: This is one of the most common words in English, but relying on it too much can make your essay sound basic. In IELTS, you often need to be more specific. Knowing synonyms shows you can choose the right word for the right context.| Synonyms: Individuals
column3: Citizens | Examples: Many people believe that the government should be solely responsible for environmental protection.
column4: The public often believes that the government should be solely responsible... (This refers to people as a collective group).
column5: Many citizens believe that the government should be solely responsible... (This is perfect when discussing rights, duties, and a person's relationship with their country).

column1: Important | Context: You will often need to argue that a point is important. However, 'important' is sometimes overused. Using stronger, more descriptive synonyms will make your arguments more persuasive. | Synonyms: Crucial
column3: Essential | Examples: It is important for young people to learn a second language.
column4: It is crucial for young people to learn a second language. (Implies it's a decisive point for future success).
column5: It is vital for young people to learn a second language. (Suggests it is necessary or essential for life/success).
column6: It is essential for young people to learn a second language. (Implies it is a fundamental, necessary component).

column1: Good | Context: 'Good' can be quite a vague adjective in academic writing. What does 'good' actually mean? Examiners look for precision. Replacing 'good' with a more descriptive word makes your writing clearer and more impactful. | Synonyms: Beneficial
column3: Favourable | Examples: Spending time in nature has a good effect on mental health.
column4: Spending time in nature has a beneficial effect on mental health. (Clearly states it provides an advantage or help).
column5: Spending time in nature has a positive effect on mental health. (A direct and academic-sounding alternative).
column6: Spending time in nature has a favourable effect on mental health. (Indicates an outcome that is advantageous).

column1: Develop | Context: This is a versatile verb used to talk about progress, growth, or creating something new. It's excellent for discussing solutions, skills, or economic changes. | Synonyms: Cultivate
column3: Establish | Examples: Countries need to develop more sustainable sources of energy.
column4: Countries need to cultivate more sustainable sources of energy. (Suggests gradual, careful development).
column5: Countries need to foster more sustainable sources of energy. (Implies encouraging something to grow).
column6: Countries need to establish more sustainable sources of energy. (Focuses on the creation and setting up of these sources).

column1: Increase (verb) | Context: Essential for both Task 1 (describing data trends) and Task 2 (discussing problems or solutions, e.g., an increase in crime or the need to increase funding). | Synonyms: Boost
column2: Raise | Examples: The government plans to increase investment in the public transport system.
column3: The government plans to boost investment in the public transport system. (A strong, dynamic word).
column4: The government plans to raise investment in the public transport system. (This would indicate that it will start a new initiative to increase investment).

column1: Government | Context: A key stakeholder in almost every IELTS common Task 2 topic (education, environment, health, etc.). You need synonyms to avoid repeating the government in every other sentence. | Synonyms: The authorities
column3: Policymakers | Examples: The government is responsible for creating policies that protect its citizens.
column4: The authorities are responsible for creating policies... (A general term for people in power).
column5: The state is responsible for creating policies... (Refers to the governing body of a nation).
column6: Policymakers are responsible for creating policies... (Focuses on the individuals and groups who create the rules).

column1: Society | Context: This word is at the heart of many essay questions about social trends, culture, and community issues | Synonyms: The community
column3: Civilisation | Examples: Modern technology has had a profound impact on the community. (Can be used for society in general, or a more local group).
column4: Modern technology has had a profound impact on the general populace. (A more formal, high-level term for the population).
column5: Modern technology has had a profound impact on modern civilisation. (A broader term, good for discussing major, long-term historical shifts).

column1: Technology | Context: A very frequent topic in IELTS. Being able to discuss it with a varied vocabulary is a huge advantage. | Synonyms: Innovation
column3: Technological progress | Examples: The advancement of technology has transformed the way we work and communicate.
column4: The advancement of innovation has transformed the way we work... (Focuses on the new ideas and inventions).
column5: Digital tools have transformed the way we work and communicate. (More specific, good when talking about computers, apps, and the internet).
column6: Technological progress has transformed the way we work and communicate. (A good phrase to use for variety).

column1: Environment | Context: Another classic IELTS topic. You need to be able to discuss nature, pollution, and conservation with academic language. | Synonyms: The natural world
column3: The planet | Examples: Protecting the environment is a challenge that requires global cooperation.
column4: Protecting the natural world is a challenge that requires global cooperation. (A lovely, descriptive alternative).
column5: Protecting our ecosystems is a challenge that requires global cooperation. (More scientific, referring to the complex network of living organisms).
column6: Protecting the planet is a challenge that requires global cooperation. (Used for emphasis on a global scale).

column1: Children | Context: Education, family, and social development are common themes. You'll need alternatives to 'children'. | Synonyms: Youngsters
column3: Young people
column4: Minors | Examples: It is vital that children are taught about healthy eating habits from a young age.
column5: It is vital that youngsters are taught about healthy eating habits...(Slightly less formal but acceptable in IELTS speaking)
column6: It is vital that the youth are taught about healthy eating habits...(Refers to this demographic group as a whole).
column7: It is vital that minors are taught about healthy eating habits... (A more formal/legal term, good for essays on law and responsibility). | Note: This is heavily dependent on context. Teenagers/Adolescents can be used for those between the ages of 13- 17, Infant for babies (less than 1 years old), Toddlers for 2- 3 year olds

column1: Students | Context: Essential for any essay on the topic of education, from primary school to university | Synonyms: Learners
column3: Scholars | Examples: Universities should provide more mental health support for their students.
column4: Universities should provide more mental health support for their learners. (A broader term that emphasizes the act of learning).
column5: Schools should provide more support for their pupils. (Typically used for school-age children, not university students).
column6: The university provides funding for its most promising scholars. (Used for high-achieving or academic students, often at a higher level).

column1: Improve | Context: A core verb for suggesting solutions or discussing positive developments.| Synonyms: Enhance
column3: Refine | Examples: Governments must take steps to improve air quality in major cities.
column4: Governments must take steps to enhance air quality... (Means to intensify or increase the quality of something).
column5: Governments must take steps to ameliorate the problem of poor air quality... (A high-level, formal word meaning 'to make something bad better').
column6: The company sought to refine its manufacturing process to reduce waste. (Means to improve by making small changes).

column1: Effective | Context: When you propose a solution in Task 2, you need to argue that it will be 'effective'. Using a range of words shows a better command of English. | Synonyms: Productive
column3: Successful | Examples: Stricter penalties are an effective deterrent to crime.
column4: Stricter penalties are a productive deterrent to crime. (Suggests it produces the desired results).
column5: Stricter penalties are a potent deterrent to crime. (A powerful word, suggesting a strong effect).
column6: Stricter penalties are a successful deterrent to crime. (Focuses on the positive outcome).

column1: Necessary | Context: Used to express that something is required or must be done. It's a strong way to frame a solution or a duty. | Synonyms: Essential
column2: Imperative | Examples: It is necessary for individuals to take personal responsibility for their health.
column3: It is essential for individuals to take personal responsibility... (Means absolutely required, fundamental).
column4: It is imperative that individuals take personal responsibility... (A very strong, formal word suggesting urgency and importance).

column1: Different | Context: Useful for making comparisons and contrasts, which is a key skill in academic writing. | Synonyms: Various
column2: Diverse | Examples: There are many different approaches to solving this problem.
column3: There are various approaches to solving this problem. (A very common and effective synonym).
column4: There are diverse approaches to solving this problem. (Emphasizes a wide range of distinct types).

column1: Work (noun) | Context: The world of work, careers, and employment is a frequent topic. | Synonyms: Employment
column3: A Profession | Examples: Finding fulfilling work is a major goal for many young adults.
column4: Finding fulfilling employment is a major goal... (A more formal term).
column5: Building a fulfilling career is a major goal... (Implies a long-term professional journey).
column6: Entering a fulfilling profession is a major goal... (Often refers to jobs requiring special training or education, like medicine or law).

column1: Companies | Context: Essential for discussing topics related to business, the economy, globalization, and marketing. | Synonyms: Corporations
column2: Businesses | Examples: Companies have a social responsibility to minimize their environmental impact.
column3: Corporations have a social responsibility... (Typically refers to large companies).
column4: Businesses have a social responsibility... (A general term for any commercial organisation).

column1: Individual (noun) | Context: A formal and academic alternative to 'person'. It's used to discuss personal responsibility, rights, and the role of single people within a society. | Synonyms: Person
column2: Citizen | Examples: The rights of the individual must be balanced against the needs of the community.
column3: The rights of each person must be balanced... (singular).
column4: The rights of the citizen must be balanced... (Emphasizes the person's role in a state).

column1: Global | Context: Topics like the environment, economy, and culture are often discussed on a global scale. This adjective is crucial. | Synonyms: Worldwide
column3: Universal | Examples: Climate change is a global issue that requires an international response.
column4: Climate change is a worldwide issue... (A direct and perfectly suitable synonym).
column5: Climate change is an international issue... (Used when comparing or involving two or more nations).
column6: The desire for peace is a universal human trait. (Means applying to everyone or everything, everywhere).

column1: Modern | Context: Used to contrast the present day with the past. It's a key word for essays on social change, tradition, and technology. | Synonyms: Contemporary
column2: Present-day | Examples: Modern lifestyles are often more sedentary than those of previous generations.
column3: Contemporary lifestyles are often more sedentary... (A more formal and academic-sounding synonym).
column4: Present-day lifestyles are often more sedentary... (A clear and effective alternative).

column1: Many | Context: A very common quantifier. While perfectly fine to use, relying on it too much can seem repetitive. Using synonyms adds academic flair. | Synonyms: Numerous
column2: A large number of | Examples: Many experts argue that automation will lead to job losses.
column3: Numerous experts argue that automation will lead to job losses. (A direct and formal synonym).
column4: A large number of experts argue that automation will lead to job losses. (A simple, effective phrase to add variety).

column1: Help (verb) | Context: A simple, common verb. In formal academic writing, it's often better to use a more sophisticated alternative to sound more authoritative. | Synonyms: Assist
column3: Support | Examples: Volunteering can help students develop a sense of responsibility.
column4: Volunteering can assist students in developing a sense of responsibility. (A more formal alternative).
column5: Volunteering can aid the development of a student's sense of responsibility. (Another formal choice, often used with 'in' or in noun form).
column6: Volunteering can support students in developing a sense of responsibility." (Implies providing a foundation or encouragement).

column1: Provide | Context: This is a strong, academic verb for talking about giving, supplying, or making something available. It's excellent for essays about solutions. | Synonyms: Supply
column3: Furnish | Examples: Libraries provide access to a vast range of information for free.
column4: Libraries supply access to a vast range of information... (A direct synonym, implies fulfilling a need).
column5: Libraries offer access to a vast range of information... (Suggests making something available for people to accept or reject).
column6: Libraries furnish the community with access to a vast range of information. (A more formal, high level verb meaning to supply or provide).

column1: Believe | Context: Used for introducing an opinion or argument. Using alternatives can show the strength of your conviction and add academic weight to your claims. | Synonyms: Argue
column2: Contend | Examples: I believe that censorship in the media is sometimes justified.
column3: I would argue that censorship in the media is sometimes justified. (Stronger, suggests you have reasons and evidence).
column4: Some contend that censorship in the media is sometimes justified. (A formal verb for putting forward an argument).

column1: Create | Context: A powerful verb for discussing the act of making or bringing something into existence, from jobs and opportunities to policies and art. | Synonyms: Generate
column2: Establish | Examples: The government should implement policies to create more jobs for young graduates.
column3: The government should implement policies to generate more jobs... (Often used for things like jobs, revenue, or electricity).
column4: The government should establish a program to create more jobs... (Implies setting something up on a firm, long-term basis).

column1: Education | Context: A core IELTS topic. You need a range of words to discuss it without sounding repetitive. | Synonyms: Schooling
column2: Learning | Examples: Access to quality education is crucial for a nation's progress.
column3: Access to quality schooling is crucial for a nation's progress. (Often refers more specifically to the instruction received at school).
column4: Lifelong learning is crucial for personal and professional development. (A broader concept that includes self-study and experience).

column1: Health | Context: Another central IELTS topic covering everything from diet and exercise to healthcare systems. | Synonyms: Well-being
column3: Medical care
column4: Healthcare | Examples: An individual's diet has a direct impact on their overall health.
column5: Well-being: An individual's diet has a direct impact on their overall well-being. (A broader term that includes mental and social health, not just physical).
column6: Physical condition: An individual's diet has a direct impact on their physical condition. (More specific to the body).
column7: Medical care / Healthcare: The government needs to invest more in public healthcare. (Refers to the system of providing medical services).

column1: Family | Context: A fundamental social unit and a common topic in questions about society, tradition, and child development. | Synonyms: The household
column2: The domestic unit | Examples: The family structure has changed significantly over the last 50 years.
column3: The average household now contains fewer people than it did 50 years ago. (Refers to the people living in one house).
column4: The domestic unit has changed significantly over the last 50 years. (A very formal, sociological term for the family/household).

column1: Teachers | Context: Essential vocabulary for any essay about education. | Synonyms: Educators
column3: Instructors | Examples: Good teachers are essential for a positive learning environment.
column4: Good educators are essential for a positive learning environment. (A broader, more formal term).
column5: Many students hire private tutors to help them with difficult subjects. (Refers to a teacher who teaches individuals or small groups).
column6: Driving instructors must be patient and clear. (Often used for someone who teaches a practical skill).

column1: Adults | Context: Used to discuss people who are no longer children, often in contrast to them. Essential for essays on education, work, and life stages. | Synonyms: Grown-ups
column2: Mature individuals | Examples: Many adults are now returning to university to gain new qualifications.
column3: Many grown-ups are now returning to university... (Slightly less formal but widely understood and acceptable).
column4: Mature individuals often have different learning needs compared to younger students. (A formal and respectful way to refer to adults).

column1: Social | Context: A key adjective describing anything related to society, community, and interaction between individuals. | Synonyms: Communal
column3: Public | Examples: The rise of social media has had a major impact on people's social lives.
column4: The rise of social media has impacted communal interaction. (Relating to a community).
column5: Societal norms have changed dramatically in recent decades. (A very formal adjective relating to society as a whole).
column6: His behaviour in a public setting was unacceptable. (Relating to people in general).

column1: Public (adjective) | Context: Used to describe services and spaces that are for the use of everyone, often provided by the government. | Synonyms: State-funded
column3: Civic
column4: Community | Examples: Investment in public transport is essential to reduce traffic congestion.
column5: Investment in state-run transport is essential... (Specifies that it is funded and operated by the state).
column6: Civic duty includes voting and respecting the law. (Relating to a city or town, and the duties of citizens).
column7: Community The community swimming pool is open to everyone. (Emphasises that it serves the local community).

column1: Local | Context: An adjective to specify a particular area, neighbourhood, or region, often in contrast to 'national' or 'global'. | Synonyms: Community-based
column3: Regional | Examples: Supporting local businesses is beneficial for the community's economy.
column4: Supporting community-based businesses is beneficial... (Emphasizes that the business is part of and serves the community).
column5: The neighbourhood watch scheme helped to reduce crime. (Relating to a specific residential area).
column6: Supporting regional businesses is beneficial for the area's economy. (Refers to a larger area or region than just a town).

column1: Time | Context: An abstract concept that is central to many essays (e.g., use of free time, changes over time). You often need phrases rather than single-word synonyms. | Synonyms: Leisure hours
column2: An era | Examples: People today seem to have less free time than in the past.
column3: People today seem to have fewer leisure hours than in the past. (Specifically refers to non-work time).
column4: In a bygone era, people had more free time. (Used to refer to a distinct period in history)

column1: Life | Context: A very broad term. Making it more specific shows a higher level of language skill. | Synonyms: Existence
column3: Lifestyles
column4: Daily routines | Examples: Technology has changed almost every aspect of modern life.
column5: Technology has changed almost every aspect of modern existence. (A more philosophical, formal term).
column6: Technology has changed almost every aspect of modern lifestyles. (Focuses on the manner in which people live).
column7: Technology has changed the daily routines of most people. (More specific to day-to-day activities).

column1: Experience (noun) | Context: Can refer to an event you've lived through or, more commonly in IELTS, the knowledge and skill gained from doing something. | Synonyms: Practical knowledge
column2: Expertise | Examples: Most employers look for candidates with practical work experience.
column3: Most employers look for candidates with practical knowledge. (Highlights the hands-on aspect)
column4: Most employers look for candidates with expertise in the field. (A stronger word, implying a high level of skill)

column1: Skill | Context: Absolutely essential for topics on education and employment.| Synonyms: Abilities
column3: Prowess | Examples: The education system should focus on teaching practical skills as well as academic knowledge.
column4: The education system should focus on teaching practical abilities... (A general synonym).
column5: The education system should focus on developing core competencies. (A formal, business-oriented word for skills needed to do a job).
column6: He was admired for his academic prowess. (A high-level word meaning great skill or expertise).

column1: Information | Context: Central to discussions about media, the internet, and education. | Synonyms: Data
column2: Knowledge | Examples: The internet allows for the instant sharing of information across the globe.
column3: The internet allows for the instant sharing of data across the globe. (Often implies raw facts, figures, and statistics).
column4: The internet allows for the instant sharing of knowledge across the globe. (Implies a more understood or organised form of information).

column1: Community | Context: Refers to a group of people living in the same place or having a particular characteristic in common. | Synonyms: Society
column3: Neighbourhood | Examples: Public libraries can be a vital hub for the local community.
column4: Public libraries are vital for a well-functioning society. (Broader, refers to people in a country or region). The local populace can benefit from public libraries. (A formal word for the inhabitants of an area). A public library can be a vital hub for the neighbourhood. (More specific to a residential area).

column1: Economic | Context: A crucial adjective for discussing topics related to the economy, finance, trade, and business | Synonyms: Financial
column2: Monetary | Examples: The government's main goal is to promote economic growth.
column3: The government's main goal is to ensure financial stability. (Relating to money management).
column4: The central bank sets monetary policy, such as interest rates. (Specifically relating to the money in a country).

column1: Personal | Context: An adjective for something relating to or affecting a single individual. Used to contrast with 'public' or 'general'. | Synonyms: Individual
column3: Subjective | Examples: The choice to have children is a deeply personal decision.
column4: The choice to have children is a deeply individual decision. (A direct and formal synonym).
column5: The choice to have children is a private matter. (Emphasizes that it is not for public interference).
column6: Art appreciation is a highly subjective experience. (Means based on personal feelings or opinions, not external facts).

column1: National | Context: Describes something relating to an entire nation, as opposed to local or global. | Synonyms: Nationwide
column2: Domestic | Examples: The country's national identity is built on its unique history and culture.
column3: The government announced a nationwide lockdown. (Means extending across the whole nation).
column4: Domestic policy focuses on issues within the country. (Used to contrast with 'foreign' or 'international').

column1: Significant | Context: A more powerful and academic word than 'important' or 'big'. It means something is large or important enough to be worthy of attention. | Synonyms: Substantial
column3: Marked | Examples: There has been a substantial increase in the use of renewable energy. (Suggests a large amount or size).
column4: There has been a considerable increase in the use of renewable energy. (Another strong word for 'large in amount').
column5: There has been a marked increase in the use of renewable energy. (Means noticeable and clear).

column1: Possible | Context: Used to talk about whether something can be done. It's key for discussing solutions in Task 2. | Synonyms: Feasible
column3: Attainable | Examples: It is possible to reduce pollution if we take collective action.
column4: It is feasible to reduce pollution if we take collective action. (Means possible and practical to do easily or conveniently).
column5: This is not a financially viable solution. (Means capable of working successfully
column6: often used in a business or practical context).
column7: Setting attainable goals is key to motivation. (Means able to be reached or achieved).

column1: Available | Context: | Synonyms: Accessible
column3: On hand | Examples: Not enough funding is available for arts and culture.
column4: Not enough funding is accessible for arts and culture. (Implies it is easy to get or use).
column5: Not enough funding is obtainable for arts and culture. (A formal word for 'able to be got').
column6: We need to use the resources we have on hand. (Means present and ready for use).

column1: Successful | Context: | Synonyms: Fruitful
column3: Thriving | Examples: The campaign to raise awareness about recycling was very successful.
column4: The discussion was very fruitful, and we came up with many new ideas. (Produces good results
column5: productive).
column6: The business became very prosperous. (Used for financial success or flourishing in general).
column7: The company is thriving in the current market. (Means growing or developing vigorously).

column1: Popular | Context: | Synonyms: Widespread
column2: Prevalent | Examples: Streaming services have become extremely popular in recent years.
column3: The use of streaming services has become widespread. (Means found or distributed over a large area or number of people).
column4: This belief is more prevalent among younger generations. (Means widespread in a particular area or at a particular time).

column1: Common | Context: | Synonyms: Widespread
column3: Frequent | Examples: Traffic congestion is a common problem in most major cities.
column4: Traffic congestion is a widespread problem in most major (Found over a large area).
column5: Smartphones have become ubiquitous in modern society. (A high level word meaning present, appearing, or found everywhere).
column6: Car accidents are a frequent occurrence on this road." (Happening often).

column1: General | Context: | Synonyms: Overall
column3: Widespread
column4: Prevailing | Examples: The general opinion is that taxes are too high. The overall opinion is that taxes are too high. (Considering everything).
column5: There is widespread agreement that taxes are too high (Held by many people).
column6: The prevailing opinion is that taxes are too high. (Means existing at a particular time current).

column1: More (comparative) | Context: | Synonyms: Greater
column3: Further | Examples: We need to put more effort into protecting the environment.
column4: We need to put greater effort into protecting the environment. (A more formal and emphatic choice).
column5: We need to provide additional funding for schools. (Used to talk about adding to what is already there).
column6: Further research is needed to confirm these findings. (Often used for abstract things like research, details, or thought).
